title: Timescales for Deep and Full Thermalization
authors: Tabea Herrmann, Felix Fritzsch, Arnd Bäcker
abstract: Isolated quantum systems typically approach thermal equilibrium as described by the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH). Going beyond this involves either higher order correlators (full thermalization) or the formation of state designs, i.e., the approach of moments of state ensembles after a projective measurement towards thermal equilibrium (deep thermalization). We compare these two extensions of ETH using extensive numerical studies within a paradigmatic model for chaotic many-body quantum dynamics. For this we find exponential relaxation for both extensions: For deep thermalization all moments relax with the same rate, which approximately equals the relaxation rate of the autocorrelation function captured by ETH. In contrast, higher order correlation functions in full thermalization approach equilibrium faster. This means that at higher orders full thermalization is faster than deep thermalization.
summary: This paper investigates whether the decay of higher-order correlations (full thermalization) and the approach of projected ensembles to Haar-random states (deep thermalization) occur at the same rate. The authors find that while both are exponential, full thermalization is faster at higher orders. The study also identifies a sub-exponential decay for the observable-independent distance between ensembles.
main_problem: Comparing the relaxation timescales of two extensions of the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH): Full Thermalization (higher-order correlation functions) and Deep Thermalization (approach of projected ensembles to state designs).
main_result: Both processes exhibit exponential relaxation, but full thermalization at higher orders is faster than deep thermalization. Additionally, the decay of the observable-independent norm distance is found to be sub-exponential.
method: Large-scale numerical simulations of a kicked Ising chain combined with theoretical frameworks including Free Probability Theory, Random Matrix Theory, and the replica picture.
model_or_system: A 1D kicked mixed-field Ising spin chain subject to periodic Floquet dynamics with random longitudinal fields, serving as a paradigmatic model for chaotic many-body quantum dynamics.
key_observables: Higher-order Out-of-Time-Order Correlators (OTOCs) $F_k(t)$, moments of the projected ensemble $\Delta_k(t)$, and the Frobenius norm distance $\delta_k(t)$.
important_parameters: Order of the correlation function/moment ($k$), subsystem size ($L_A$), total system size ($L$), and the chaotic/ergodic regime.
main_assumptions: The system is in a chaotic/ergodic regime where the system acts as its own bath; the study focuses on the infinite temperature limit or pure states.
figures_summary: Figures show the time dependence of $F_k(t)$ and $\Delta_k(t)$ for different orders, the scaling of thermalization rates with system size, and the comparison between exponential decay of moments versus sub-exponential decay of the norm distance.
paper_structure: The paper defines the mathematical distinction between full and deep thermalization, presents a case study using the kicked Ising chain to extract relaxation rates, analyzes the scaling of these rates with system size and order $k$, and uses Random Matrix Theory to explain late-time fluctuations and symmetry effects.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into the hierarchy of thermalization timescales in many-body systems, which is crucial for understanding how information scrambles and how local subsystems reach equilibrium in quantum chaotic dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time dependence of full thermalization Fk, Eq. (10), and deep thermalization ∆k, Eq. (13), in the kicked field Ising model averaged over 150 spin chain realizations. The numerical data for one Gell-Mann matrix is shown for L = 12, 16, 20 (light to dark colors) and LA = 2. Bold lines show the exponential behavior found by a median regression in the corresponding time interval for all Gell-Mann matrices.
FIG. 2. Saturation values of (a) full thermalization F sat

FIG. 3. Time dependence of (a) full thermalization Fk, Eq. (10), and (b) deep thermalization ∆k, Eq. (13), in the kicked field Ising model averaged over 150 spin chain realiza- tions. The numerical data for the 15 non-trivial Gell-Mann matrices corresponding to LA = 2 is shown for L = 20 and k = 1. Bold lines show the exponential behavior found by a median regression in the corresponding time interval for all non-trivial Gell-Mann matrices.

FIG. 5. Thermalization rates of (a) full thermalization γF and (b) deep thermalization γ∆. Similar to Fig. 4 but here for LA = 1, i.e. Pauli matrices, for L = 8, . . . , 18 (150 spin chain realizations) and L = 20 (100 realizations). Data is shown for k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

FIG. 6. Thermalization rates of (a) full thermalization γF and (b) deep thermalization γ∆for Gell-Mann matrices with LA = 2 (red plus) and Pauli matrices LA = 1 (blue cross). For the case of deep thermalization we also show for even k and LA = 2 results of mean regression in early time interval t ∈[2, 7] (pink square). For k = 1, 2, 3, 4 we show L = 20 for k = 5, 6 L = 18.
